content:
# 2 Timothy Chapter 1 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.1 — "according to the promise of the life" vs. "sent out to tell others about the life he has promised"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "according to [the] promise of the life which [is] in Christos Yeshua" | kat' epangelian zōēs tēs en Christō Iēsou |
| LOGOS | "according to the promise of the life that is in Christ Jesus" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "I have been sent out to tell others about the life he has promised through faith in Christ Jesus" | Restructures; adds "sent out to tell others"; "through faith" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul's apostleship is "according to the promise of life in Christ Jesus" — a qualifying phrase describing the basis of his commission. PNEUMA restructures into a purpose clause ("sent out to tell others") and adds "through faith" — neither in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA restructures and adds.

---

### v.6 — "the gift of God" vs. "the spiritual gift God gave you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the gift of God which is in you" | to charisma tou theou |
| LOGOS | "the gift of God, which is in you" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the spiritual gift God gave you when I laid my hands on you" | Adds "spiritual"; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says "the gift (charisma) of God." PNEUMA adds "spiritual" — while charisma is indeed a spiritual gift, Paul does not use the adjective here.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "spiritual."

---

### v.7 — "a spirit of cowardice" vs. "a spirit of fear" vs. "a spirit of fear and timidity"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a spirit of cowardice" | pneuma deilias |
| LOGOS | "a spirit not of fear" | "Fear" for deilia |
| PNEUMA | "a spirit of fear and timidity" | Doubles: "fear and timidity" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. deilia means "cowardice/timidity/fear." LOGOS renders as "fear." PNEUMA doubles to "fear and timidity" — two words for one Greek term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA doubles.

---

### v.9 — "before times eternal" vs. "before the ages began" vs. "before the beginning of time"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "before times eternal" | pro chronōn aiōniōn |
| LOGOS | "before the ages began" | "Ages began" — temporal |
| PNEUMA | "before the beginning of time — to show us his grace through Christ Jesus" | "Beginning of time"; adds "to show us his grace" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. pro chronōn aiōniōn means "before eternal times/ages." LOGOS renders well. PNEUMA adds "to show us his grace through Christ Jesus" — expanding the purpose beyond what Paul states here.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds purpose clause.

---

### v.10 — "abolished the death" vs. "abolished death" vs. "broke the power of death"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who indeed abolished the death" | katargēsantos men ton thanaton |
| LOGOS | "who abolished death" | Same — drops article |
| PNEUMA | "He broke the power of death" | "Broke the power of" — paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. katargeō means "to render inoperative/abolish/nullify." LOGOS preserves the verb's force. PNEUMA's "broke the power of" is interpretively correct but adds "power" — Paul says Christ abolished death itself, not merely its power.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "power of."

---

### v.12 — "my deposit" vs. "what has been entrusted to me" vs. "what I have entrusted to him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he is able to guard my deposit until that day" | tēn parathēkēn mou — "my deposit" |
| LOGOS | "he is able to guard until that day what has been entrusted to me" | Passive — deposit entrusted to Paul |
| PNEUMA | "he is able to guard what I have entrusted to him until the day of his return" | Active — Paul entrusts to God; adds "of his return" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. tēn parathēkēn mou is genuinely ambiguous: "my deposit" could mean (a) what was entrusted to me (the gospel), or (b) what I entrusted to him (my life/ministry). LOGOS reads as passive (a); PNEUMA reads as active (b). Both are defensible. PNEUMA also adds "of his return" — Paul says only "that day."

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. The direction of the deposit — God→Paul or Paul→God — affects whether Paul expresses confidence in the gospel's preservation or in God's custody of his life. The ambiguity may be intentional, encompassing both.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 3 | "Spiritual" (v.6), "to show us his grace" (v.9), "of his return" (v.12) |
| PNEUMA doubles terms | 1 | "Fear and timidity" (v.7) |
| PNEUMA paraphrase | 1 | "Broke the power of death" for "abolished death" (v.10) |
| Deposit direction | 1 | God→Paul vs. Paul→God (v.12) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 1's most significant disagreement is the direction of the "deposit" (parathēkē) in v.12 — is Paul confident that God will guard the gospel entrusted to Paul, or that God will guard Paul's life entrusted to God? The ambiguity is theologically rich, and the translators' choices reveal different readings. PNEUMA also softens "abolished death" to "broke the power of death" — Paul's claim is more radical: death itself is rendered null.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
